title: Wedging clay, the spiral method
subtitle: How to prepare clay for every throw or hand-build that follows
excerpt: Wedging is the kneading-of-clay that every throwing or hand-building tutorial assumes you have already done. The spiral method (also called ram's-head wedging) compresses the clay, evens the moisture, and aligns the particles into a workable consistency. Twenty minutes of practice gives a foundation skill every other pottery tutorial builds on.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (c. 1885), § Preparation of Clay for the Wheel: documents the spiral / ram's-head wedging method as it is still taught. Bernard Leach, A Potter's Book (1940), § Throwing: places wedging as the non-negotiable preparation step before any throwing work, with the spiral version explicitly named as the British studio default. The method itself is documented across every major potters' tradition; the spiral and ox-head names are interchangeable, used in different studios.

content.text: Every throwing tutorial and every hand-building tutorial assumes the clay has been
wedged
before the reader starts. Wedging is the kneading-of-clay that prepares the body: it pushes out any
air pockets

, evens the moisture content from drier outer layers and wetter inner ones, and aligns the particle structure into a workable consistency. Unwedged clay throws unevenly and blows out during firing; wedged clay throws smoothly and fires clean. The practice cost: twenty minutes of physical work before each pot, less once the rhythm is in the hands.

This guide covers the spiral method, also called ram's-head wedging. It is the default in studios in Britain and Japan. The alternative cut-and-slap method splits the clay in half with a wire and slams the halves back together. It is faster, but in a hurry it traps more air than it gets out. Spiral wedging is the safe default for beginners. It remains the standard for studio work. Both finish at the same consistency.

attrs.body: Wet-clean the wedging board between sessions; never sweep dry clay residue.
attrs.title: Clay dust note

content.text: What you need
A waist-height wedging surface

. A thick plywood board (18 mm or more) clamped to a sturdy table works as a starter setup. A purpose-built plaster bat absorbs water as you wedge, which helps slightly-wet clay, but plain wood is fine for everyday wedging.
Clay to wedge

. A 1-2 kg block is the right size to learn on. Earthenware, stoneware, or porcelain, any throwing-track clay body wedges the same way. Don't try to wedge polymer or air-dry on a wedging surface; those bodies don't take the spiral motion and aren't intended for it.
A wire cutter
. For slicing the finished wedged clay to check for air pockets at the end of the session.
Apron and old clothes
. Wedging splatters slightly with wetter clay; not enough to stain a kitchen surface, plenty to mark a good shirt.
Method
1. Set up the body position

Stand at the wedging surface with feet shoulder-width apart, weight slightly forward on the balls of the feet, knees soft (not locked). The wedging board should sit at the height where your elbows hang relaxed by your sides; the heel of the hand rests on the clay with the forearm at roughly 30° down from horizontal. If you find yourself leaning over or stretching up, the surface is wrong for your body, raise or lower it before continuing.
2. Cone the clay

Take the 1-2 kg block of clay and shape it roughly into a cone or fat tear-drop, with the broad end on the wedging surface and the narrow point upward. Use both hands to compress and shape the clay; don't be precious about it. The starting cone shape gives the spiral motion something to work with, wedging an irregularly-shaped block is harder and slower.
3. The spiral push

Place the heels of both hands at the upper end of the clay cone, with the fingers curving down over the broader base. With weight from the hips and shoulders (not from the arms alone), push down and slightly forward, rolling the clay over the upper face. The cone deforms into a curved spiral shape; the front face folds under as the upper face presses down.

After the first push, rock back and rotate the clay slightly (about 30° to your dominant-hand side). Push down again with the same hip-and-shoulder weight. Each push compresses a fresh sector of the clay; each rotation moves the next sector into position. The clay walks slowly in a spiral around the same centre point on the wedging board.
4. The ram's-head shape develops

After 15-20 pushes, the clay takes on a recognisable shape, a curved swirl with a hooked upper edge that looks like a ram's head or an ammonite shell. This is the working shape that gives the technique its other name. The visible spiral on the surface is the trace of the rotation, with each fold compressing fresh clay underneath the previous one.

Keep the rhythm: push, rock back, rotate, push. Don't pause; the rhythm is part of the work. A wedging session for 1-2 kg of clay is 50-80 pushes, about 5-10 minutes of continuous motion, depending on the clay body and how recently it was last wedged. Stoneware wedges in fewer pushes than porcelain; freshly-bought block clay takes longer than reclaimed studio clay that's been wedged before.
5. Check the consistency

After 5-10 minutes, the clay should feel uniform under the hands, no firm spots, no soft spots, no visible variation in colour or texture. A wedged stoneware feels like a firm pillow; porcelain feels slightly more slippery; earthenware sits between the two.
If the clay is approaching
throwing consistency

, it should hold a fingerprint clearly without the print closing up after a few seconds. If the print closes (clay too wet), wedge for a few more minutes on a drier portion of the board to lose moisture. If the print won't form (clay too dry), wrap in a damp cloth for 30 minutes and re-wedge briefly afterwards.
6. The cut test

Before committing to a throw, do the cut test. Take the wire cutter and slice the wedged clay in half through its widest cross-section. Look at both cut faces: the clay should be uniformly coloured and textured with no visible air bubbles. Any pinhole-sized or larger bubble is a wedging failure, the clay needs another 5-10 minutes of spiral wedging. Don't ignore visible bubbles; they cause blow-outs in the kiln.

Re-form the cut halves into a single block by pressing them firmly back together with the cut faces aligned, then continue wedging if more is needed. The cut test is the final quality check before the clay is ready to throw or hand-build with.
What good wedging feels like

The rhythm is the experienced potter's tell. Push, rock, rotate in a steady pulse like a slow heartbeat. Your hands feel the clay change as you wedge. A stiff fresh block softens a little under repeated compression. A too-wet block tightens a little as moisture spreads out. A well-wedged block feels evenly firm to the push, with no soft spots.

Body mechanics tell on the next morning. Soreness in the shoulders and upper back is normal in the first weeks of regular wedging; the muscles strengthen. Soreness in the wrists, forearms, or fingers is wrong, that pattern means the rotation is coming from the lower body rather than the hips and shoulders. If wrist pain develops, stop wedging for a few days and watch a coach or video reference for body-mechanics correction before continuing.
When to wedge, and how much
Fresh from the bag

. A new block of bagged clay is reasonably well-wedged from the manufacturer but still benefits from a 5-minute spiral pass to settle the body. Skip this only if pushed for time on small (<500 g) projects.
Reclaimed clay

. Studio clay reclaimed from trimmings or failed pots needs full wedging, 10-15 minutes of spiral on a 1-2 kg block. Reclaimed clay carries variable moisture, embedded slurry, and air pockets from the reclaim process.
Between throws

. On the wheel, a brief 2-3 minute re-wedge of a 500 g ball is the working compromise, enough to refresh the body, not enough to stop the throwing flow. Some throwers re-wedge between every pot; others only between batches.
For hand-building

. A 5-minute spiral on the lump you'll use is usually enough. Pinch pots and small slab work tolerate slightly-less-wedged clay than throwing does, the slow forming gives air pockets time to migrate out under hand pressure.
If the wedging went sideways

attrs.intro: Four things that go wrong on first wedging sessions and the fix for each.

items.fix: Stop wedging for a day or two. Re-watch a coach demonstrating the body mechanics; the cue is that the hands act as transmission rather than as primary muscle. The rotation comes from rotating the upper body at the hips, not from twisting the wrists. Stand further back from the board if you find yourself wrist-deep in the clay.
items.cause: Wedging is being done from the wrists rather than from the hips and shoulders.
items.symptom: Wrist or forearm pain after a wedging session

items.fix: Wrap the clay in a damp cloth for 30 minutes, or knead in a few drops of water. Re-wedge. Wet hands during the wedging help slightly. If the clay is actually too dry to recover (cracking under hand pressure rather than yielding), it's beyond a working session, reclaim by soaking overnight in a bucket of water.
items.cause: Clay too dry; the rotation can't bridge fresh pushes onto the previous ones.
items.symptom: Clay won't form a stable spiral, keeps falling apart into separate pieces

items.fix: Re-shape into a clean cone before continuing. Widen the rotation so each push lands on a fresh sector; the centre of the spiral should be stable but every push outside the centre should land on previously-unworked clay. Cut and check again after another 5 minutes.
items.cause: Either the spiral rotation is too narrow (only compressing the same sector repeatedly) or the cone wasn't shaped at the start.
items.symptom: Cut test reveals air bubbles even after 15 minutes of wedging

items.fix: Stop wedging. Wet-clean the surface with a damp cloth or sponge to remove all dried clay. The dried scraps embed into fresh clay and show as hard inclusions on the thrown pot; they also pose a silica-dust risk if dry-swept. Never use a dry brush or compressed air on a wedging surface.
items.cause: Wedging surface was not cleaned between sessions.
items.symptom: Wedging board has dried up clay scraps that scratch fresh clay
